# COMP110 Data Analysis Project

## Summary of the Analysis

For this project, I analyzed whether help sessions in COMP110 should be made mandatory in order to improve student understanding. I focused on the relationship between office hour visits, students' ratings of office hour effectiveness, self-reported understanding, and course difficulty. My goal was to see whether students who use help sessions more, or find them more effective, also report stronger understanding of course concepts.

## Visualization 1: Understanding by Office Hour Visits

<img src="{{ '/static/imgs/understanding_by_oh_visits.png' | relative_url }}" alt="Boxplot of understanding by number of office hour visits" width="500">

This chart shows how students’ self-reported understanding changes across different numbers of office hour visits.

## Visualization 2: Understanding vs. Perceived Office Hour Effectiveness

<img src="{{ '/static/imgs/oh_effectiveness_vs_understanding.png' | relative_url }}" alt="Scatterplot of understanding versus perceived office hour effectiveness" width="500">

This chart shows the relationship between how effective students think office hours are and how well they feel they understand the course material.

## Visualization 3: Difficulty by Office Hour Visits

<img src="{{ '/static/imgs/difficulty_by_oh_visits.png' | relative_url }}" alt="Bar chart of difficulty by office hour visits" width="500">

This chart shows that students who attend office hours more often also tend to report higher difficulty in the course.

## Final Conclusion

The data gave mixed evidence for the idea that help sessions should be mandatory for all students. Students who thought office hours were more effective tended to report better understanding, which suggests that help sessions are valuable. However, students who attended office hours more often also tended to report lower understanding and higher difficulty, which suggests that these students may already be struggling.

Because of this, I would not recommend making help sessions mandatory for everyone. A better idea would be to encourage or require help sessions mainly for students who are struggling, while also improving the quality and accessibility of help resources.
